front: Poem: villanelle that advises the reader to "practice losing farther, losing faster" and lists progressive losses from door keys to continents
Poem: ends with the parenthetical command "(Write it!)" after cataloging losses including "my mother's watch" and "two cities, lovely ones"
Poem: last stanza uses "the joking voice, a gesture I love" and references Casa Mariana as "one of three loved houses"
Poem: describes "frail, illegal fire balloons" climbing a mountain near Rio de Janeiro and an owl's nest catching fire
Poem: ends with image of "a weak mailed fist / clenched ignorant against the sky"
Poem: describes the "kite sticks of the Southern Cross" visible in the night sky
Poem: ends with oil spreading until "everything / was rainbow, rainbow, rainbow!" as the speaker releases the catch from a "little rented boat"
Poem: describes a creature whose "brown skin hung in strips / like ancient wallpaper" with a "five-haired beard of wisdom"
Poem: a draft was sent to the speaker's mentor, who called it "a real 'trifle'" and "very bad... perhaps like Ernest Hemingway!"
Poem: speaker reads National Geographic in a dentist's office while waiting for Aunt Consuelo, sees "horrifying" photographs of breasts
Poem: repeats the date "February 1918"
Poem: speaker recalls a "sensation of falling off the round, turning world"
Poem: grandmother sings to the "Marvel Stove" as a child draws houses with crayons
Poem: an almanac says "Time to plant tears"
Poem: title inspired by newspaper misprint of "mammoth"
Poem: title creature thinks the moon is a small hole in the sky and rides the subway
Poem: title creature drops a tear that is "pure enough to drink" and is frightened of a third rail during nightly subway voyages
Poem: speaker says of a companion: "If only he had been a woman!" — that companion dies of measles on an island with "fifty-two / miserable, small volcanoes"
Poem: opens "Oh, but it is dirty!" and ends "Somebody loves us all"
Poem: cans are arranged to spell "ESSO-SO-SO-SO"
Poem: set in a "cold, cold parlor" with a stuffed loon on a marble-topped table
back: First Death in Nova Scotia (Elizabeth Bishop)

front: Poem: a dead child is "all white, like a doll that hadn't been painted yet"
back: First Death in Nova Scotia (Elizabeth Bishop)

front: Poem: based on visits to an institutionalized poet
Poem: modeled on "This is the House that Jack Built"
Poem: ends every stanza with "the man that lies in the house of Bedlam"
Author: lived in Brazil for nearly two decades with partner Lota de Macedo Soares, who was the dedicatee of Questions of Travel
Author: correspondence with Robert Lowell collected in Words in Air
Author: adapted into a play by Sarah Ruhl
Author: translated Carlos Drummond de Andrade, Clarice Lispector, and Octavio Paz into English during time in Brazil
Author: rebuked a friend for publishing his ex-wife's private letters in his collection The Dolphin, stating "art just isn't worth that much"
Author: argued with mentor about vulgarity of "water-closet" in a poem about a statue of Christ and St. Peter with a rooster
Poem: opens "Land lies in water; it is shadowed green" — "The names of seashore towns run out to sea"
Poem: speaker describes "cold dark deep and absolutely clear" water — "it is like what we imagine knowledge to be"
Poem: invites a fellow poet to "please come flying"
back: Invitation to Miss Marianne Moore (Elizabeth Bishop)

front: Poem: recounts their first meeting at the stone lions of the New York Public Library
back: Invitation to Miss Marianne Moore (Elizabeth Bishop)

front: Poem: says everything in the Bible is "only connected by 'and' and 'and'"
back: Over 2,000 Illustrations and a Complete Concordance (Elizabeth Bishop)
work: Over 2,000 Illustrations and a Complete Concordance

front: Poem: calls The Seven Wonders of the World "tired / and a touch familiar"
back: Over 2,000 Illustrations and a Complete Concordance (Elizabeth Bishop)
